doc_title: Validation of U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Environmenatl Sampling Techniques that Support the Detection and Recovery of Microorganisms

steps.text: Establish appropriate safety and health practices prior to adopting a sampling technique.
Develop a safety plan and observe all safety procedures within the plan.
Develop a security plan that includes the site, staff, storage, and transport of potentially hazardous materials.
Establish a management system in place that accurately reflects the operating and QA/QC programs in the laboratory.
Prepare a sampling plan in advance for all sampling programs.
Perform field measurements such as pH and temperature on a separate subsample.
Discard the subsample used for field measurements to avoid contaminating samples for laboratory analysis.
Clean, sterilize, and prove reusable containers free of quantifiable target analyte before use.
Place collected samples in sealed containers to prevent contamination during storage and/or transport.
Document the transfer of samples and data from one person to another on chain-of-custody forms.
Classify samples collected and wastes generated during field investigations or in response to hazardous material incidents prior to shipment.
Package, label, and ship potentially hazardous materials according to the appropriate ground or air regulations.
Prepare a suitable validation report for placing in the public docket.
Peer review EPA sampling techniques prior to publication.

preconditions.text: The corresponding health and safety plan must be approved by the local Safety, Health and Environmental Management (SHEM) office or the chemical hygiene officer before starting a project.
Before sampling or analytical work begins, a management system should be in place.
Prior to shipment, samples collected and wastes generated must be classified by certified DOT/IATA personnel.

constraints.text: Live organisms such as pathogenic microorganisms create potentially high biohazard and infection risks.
Use sterile gloves and other necessary techniques, such as washing hands and wearing facemasks, to reduce sample contamination.
The sample container’s inner portion should not be touched or handled by the operator.
Personnel with approved DOT/IATA training must perform all shipments of potentially hazardous materials.
